title: Fixes #36590 - subclasses of BaseCache should use specialized implementation of methods for async version

issues.body:
These tests will pass even without your changes applied as these methods are always present they were just the wrong implementations. You could assert against their nature instead and the test doesn't need to be `async`

```suggestion
    def test_async_impl(self):
        if cache.get_many is n

+from asgiref.sync import sync_to_async

 from django.conf import settings

issues.body: This should be defined on `BaseCache` and not `BaseDatabaseCache`. As pointed out in the ticket the memcached and redis backend are also affected.

issues.body: Based on ticket comments, I had thought the goal was to only apply this change to subclasses of BaseDatabaseCache.  Moving these to BaseCache is ultimately cleaner.
issues.diff_hunk:
@@ -39,6 +53,18 @@ class CacheEntry:
 
         self.cache_model_class = CacheEntry
 
+    def __init_subclass__(cls, **kwargs):
+        # If a subcl

issues.body: Adding these directly to BaseCache vs trying to apply them conditionally via __init_subclass__ is ultimately cleaner.

         self.cache_model_class = CacheEntry
 
+    def __init_subclass__(cls, **kwargs):
+        # If a subcl

issues.body: The object ID ultimately doesn't match, so an is comparison fails.  I've settled on comparing pickles of the methods and asserting that calling the async method calls the sync version.

issues.body:
I think we also need to do something similar for `aclose`, `aincr`, `ahas_key` as these are overwritten

But I also like Simon's ticket suggestion that we should add checks for whether the function has been overridden e.g.
```python
        if self.get_many.__func__ is not BaseCache.get_many:
Apologies, I thought I'd taken that out.

issues.body:
All of the BaseCache async methods should now be consistently implemented.  

Now that this is implemented directly in BaseCache (vs in conditional logic in `__init_subclass__`), is this check still necessary?  I've added a test case that asserts the expected inheritance.
Yes, because while this PR fixes an inheritance problem, it takes a step backwards in the async implementations for all cases where these are not overwritten
I would suggest a more verbose test method name. You could do away with the comment in that case.
This is always False, so the assertions aren't running.
